Security Considerations for Web Service Call-In

When Uniface is configured to allow web service call-in, the web server is available to receive HTTP requests. Depending on its location, it could be available to anyone on the internet. Even if it is behind a firewall, it is still vulnerable to attacks over the intranet.

When you implement web service call-in, you make a Uniface execution environment available to web SOAP requests. Normally, you make a WSDL file available to those applications to which you wish to give access, giving the application information about the services and operations that you wish to make available externally.

Threats

By default, any Uniface service that is available to the Uniface Server can be called via a correctly formatted SOAP message. An attacker with detailed knowledge of the service, its operations, and parameters could correctly format the SOAP request, and it will be executed, even if you did not make the WSDL available or did not intend the service to be public. For more information, see Data I/O for Web Applications.

Defenses

There are several ways to protect your environment from divulging possibly confidential information. You can:

  • Limit the access to your web server to the required level. If you only need to make information available on the intranet, make sure that it is behind a firewall. If only certain users are allowed, protect you web server with user name and password combinations.
  • Limit the services available to the Uniface Server to only those that you want to make public.
  • Implement a finer granularity of security using the SOAP call-in callback functions. The SOAP_CALLIN_PRE function receives the server variables from the (Tomcat) web server. These are the same variables that are available in $webinfo("WEBSERVERCONTEXT"). This allows you to program your own access rights depending on the user and the host of the requester.
  • Use a public soap declaration in the operations that you want to make available for call-in . For more information, see soap.
Related Links
  • $webinfo ("WebServerContext")

    The WebServerContext topic contains an associative list of properties that provide state information about the Uniface Server and the context in which it is running.

  • SOAP Callback Operations

    SOAP callback operations are pre-defined operations that are called before or after a SOAP request is handled for web services call-in or call-out. The callback operations make it possible to add or interpret SOAP header elements, encrypt or decrypt parts of the SOAP envelope, provide SOAP fault error handling, or perform other desired processing of the SOAP envelope.
